channel.title: Psychology Today – Latest
channel.description: Latest articles from Psychology Today (full content)

item.title: You Are Not a Project to Be Improved
item.description:
Anxiety

You Are Not a Project to Be Improved

The pressure to improve everything may be a response to deeper uncertainty and disconnection

Posted April 11, 2026

Key points
  • In an unpredictable world, self-improvement can become a way to manage anxiety.
  • Self-awareness can become self-surveillance.
  • A constant drive toward improvement can pull us away from connection.
Source: Designed by Freepik

I track my steps, my workouts, my calories. I read the research, listen to podcasts, and try to stay current on what it means to live well. Recently, after watching yet another segment on food, health, and longevity, I found myself saying, “I’m tired of tracking and managing everything.”

It wasn’t dramatic. In fact, it felt like a quiet realization that had been slowly building. But it stayed with me, and beneath the routines and metrics was a deeper question: When did caring for ourselves begin to feel like perfecting ourselves?

When Care Becomes Self-Surveillance

There is nothing inherently problematic about wanting to grow or take care of ourselves. Yet over time, it can begin to feel like pressure—a quiet sense that there is a version of ourselves we are supposed to be moving toward, but never quite reaching.

The tools we use, and the culture surrounding them, can shift how we relate to that process. Emerging research on digital monitoring and wearable technologies suggests that while these tools can increase awareness, they may also heighten self-evaluation and psychological vigilance (Jafarlou et al., 2024). What begins as helpful information can become a constant internal assessment.

The question subtly shifts from "How am I doing?" to "Am I doing this right?" For many, that question is difficult to answer.

The Hidden Cost of Perfection

What I have been noticing isn’t just fatigue, it’s disconnection. When we begin to relate to ourselves primarily through the lens of improvement, we can lose access to something more essential: presence, embodiment, and even compassion. At the same time, we are facing a growing crisis of loneliness. Public health data continues to link loneliness and lack of social support with poorer mental health outcomes (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, 2024), and recent meta-analytic work confirms its impact on both psychological and physical health (Zheng et al., 2025).

These patterns are not unrelated.

As we focus more on managing ourselves, we find ourselves less connected to one another. Technology can help us track our behaviors, but it cannot replace the regulating presence of another person. Even advances in digital tools designed to detect loneliness highlight this tension, as connection is measured but often not meaningfully experienced (Jafarlou et al., 2024).

In this way, the pursuit of perfection can quietly become a substitute for connection.

What We May Really Be Seeking

It is worth asking a more compassionate question: What are we trying to steady through all of this effort? For many, it is not simply about health or discipline. It is about anxiety. Uncertainty. The desire to feel anchored in a world that often feels unsteady.

Research on self-compassion offers a meaningful counterpoint. Treating ourselves with kindness rather than constant evaluation is associated with greater psychological well-being (Li et al., 2024) and even brief interventions can significantly improve emotional health (Han et al., 2024).

These findings suggest something both simple and profound: Well-being is shaped not only by what we do, but by how we relate to ourselves, especially when we fall short of our own expectations.

A Different Way of Being

As a professor of psychology and a person of faith, I find myself returning to a quieter truth: Our worth was never meant to be achieved; it is inherent.

This does not diminish the value of growth or care for our bodies and minds. But it reframes the purpose beneath those efforts. Within the Christian tradition, the idea that we are created with inherent dignity offers a powerful counterpoint to performance-based identity. We are not valuable because we perfect ourselves and perform. We are valuable because we exist.

Psychologically, this aligns with a growing emphasis on compassion, integration, and relational well-being. The shift is subtle, but meaningful: From perfection to presence, from evaluation to awareness, and from control to connection.

I still wear the watch. I still pay attention to my health. But I am learning to hold these practices more lightly, to let them support my life, rather than define it.

In a world that feels increasingly unstable, we will be tempted to steady ourselves by trying to get everything right. But healing may ask something different of us: not perfection, but connection.

Read the original article on Psychology Today →
item.dc:creator: Kristen Dial, Psy.D.
